source: Fig1 red arrow Acute Threat->Fear + p.4-5 'Acute Threat...robust associations with...HiTOP Fear, a subfactor of Internalizing'
verification_citation: de Looff et al. 2021; PMID 34774587; doi:10.1016/j.neubiorev.2021.11.003
verification_note: Heightened autonomic/threat reactivity tracks fear-anxiety symptomatology; direction consistent with elevated acute-threat response in fear disorders.

source: Fig1 red arrow Potential Threat->Fear + p.5 'association between RDoC Potential Threat and HiTOP Fear'
verification_note: Heightened anticipatory threat reactivity (e.g. ERN/worry) associated with anxiety/fear psychopathology; same positive direction.

source: p.5 'differential association of RDoC Potential Threat with the HiTOP Fear and Distress subfactors...positive relationship with HiTOP Distress' (text only; not endpoint-distinct in Fig1)
verification_note: Anticipatory threat sensitivity (worry) linked to distress dimension; positive direction supported indirectly.

source: Fig1 red arrow Sustained Threat->Distress + p.5 'Sustained Threat appears robustly linked with HiTOP Distress'
verification_note: Chronic stress/sustained threat exposure prospectively elevates depression-anxiety (distress); positive direction consistent.

source: Fig1 red arrow Loss->Distress + p.5 'Loss also appears robustly linked with HiTOP Distress'
verification_note: Loss/negative-valence reactivity central to depression (distress); reward-loss neuroimaging in depression supports the link.

source: p.5 'emerging links of RDoC Frustrative Non-Reward...with the HiTOP Distress subfactor of the Internalizing spectrum' (Fig1 shows no arrow - emerging)
verification_note: No independent meta-analytic source located linking frustrative nonreward specifically to the Distress subfactor.

source: p.5 'and the HiTOP Antisocial Behavior subfactor of the Disinhibited and Antagonistic Externalizing spectra' (aggression; Fig1 shows no arrow - emerging)
verification_citation: de Looff et al. 2021; PMID 34774587; doi:10.1016/j.neubiorev.2021.11.003
verification_note: Frustration/reactive aggression maps onto antisocial-aggressive behavior; direction consistent with reactive-aggression literature.

source: p.5 '...Antisocial Behavior subfactor of the Disinhibited and Antagonistic Externalizing spectra' (aggression; Fig1 shows no arrow - emerging)
verification_citation: de Looff et al. 2021; PMID 34774587; doi:10.1016/j.neubiorev.2021.11.003
verification_note: Frustration-driven aggression linked to antagonistic/antisocial behavior; same positive direction.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Reward Anticipation->Substance Abuse + p.5 'Reward Anticipation...associated with...Disinhibited Externalizing...Substance Abuse...atypical/reduced anticipatory signaling in addiction'
verification_note: fMRI meta-analysis: addiction shows decreased striatal activation during reward anticipation; matches reduced-function (-1) direction.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Reward Anticipation->Distress + p.5-6 'decreased...MID-related anticipatory reward processing...with depressed mood...emerging link to HiTOP Distress subfactor'
verification_note: Neuroimaging meta-analysis: depression shows decreased thalamic/striatal activation during monetary reward anticipation; matches -1.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Reward Anticipation->Thought Disorder + p.5 'linked to the HiTOP Thought Disorder spectrum, as reduced anticipatory signaling...associated with negative symptoms of schizophrenia'
verification_note: Reduced reward/motivation processing associated with negative symptoms of schizophrenia (Thought Disorder); same -1 direction.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Initial Response to Reward->Distress + p.6 'robust link to the HiTOP Distress subfactor, based on...depressed mood with blunted ERPs'
verification_note: Depression shows decreased sgACC/striatal reactivity during reward receipt (hedonic response); matches blunted initial response (-1).

source: Fig2 blue arrow Initial Response to Reward->Substance Abuse + p.6 'link...HiTOP Substance Abuse subfactor...reduced activations in OFC, amygdala, striatum...related to addiction'
verification_note: Addiction reward-processing disruption (reward-deficiency); anticipation hypoactivation supports reduced-function direction, though outcome-phase striatum can be hyperactive.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Reinforcement Learning->Distress + p.6 'association with the HiTOP Distress subfactor...blunted reward learning...in distress disorders'
verification_note: Depression shows blunted reward-related striatal reactivity underpinning impaired reward learning; matches -1 direction.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Reinforcement Learning->Substance Abuse + p.6 'link to the HiTOP Substance Abuse subfactor...disrupted reward learning...with addiction'
verification_note: Addiction associated with disrupted striatal reward learning signals; consistent with impaired-function direction.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Reinforcement Learning->Thought Disorder + p.6 'association, although potentially weaker, with the HiTOP Thought Disorder spectrum...abnormal neural responses...with psychotic symptoms'
verification_note: Impaired reward/effort learning relates to schizophrenia negative symptoms; same -1 direction (weaker).

source: Fig2 red arrow Reward Prediction Error->Substance Abuse + p.6 'robustly linked to the HiTOP Substance Abuse subfactor...increased activity of dopaminergic neurons...increased craving'
verification_note: Substance addiction shows increased ventral striatal activation during reward outcome; consistent with elevated outcome/PE signaling (+1).

source: Fig2 red arrow Reward Prediction Error->Antisocial Behavior + p.6 'link...with the HiTOP Antisocial Behavior subfactor...aberrant dopaminergic functioning'
verification_note: No independent meta-analysis located on reward prediction error specifically with antisocial behavior.

source: p.6 'reduced activity in the dopaminergic frontal, striatal, and limbic system underlying prediction error...associated with psychotic symptoms...emerging link to HiTOP Thought Disorder' (Fig2 shows red arrows to Externalizing only)
verification_note: Aberrant/reduced reward-PE signaling tied to psychotic (esp. negative) symptoms; consistent with -1 for Thought Disorder.

source: Fig2 red arrow Habit->Substance Abuse + p.6 'linked to the HiTOP Substance Abuse subfactor, as...substance-related habits...robustly associated with increased activity in dorsal striatum'
verification_note: Addiction involves dorsal-striatal habit/outcome hyperactivity; consistent with increased habit signaling (+1).

source: Fig2 blue arrow Reward Probability->Antisocial Behavior + p.6 'robust link with the HiTOP Antisocial Behavior subfactor, as externalizing behavior...associated with impaired probability discounting'
verification_note: Externalizing/impulsive choice (impaired probabilistic valuation) linked to antisocial-spectrum behavior; same -1 direction.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Reward Probability->Substance Abuse + p.6 'additional link...to the HiTOP Substance Abuse subfactor via a short-term probability discounting process, though findings are mixed'
verification_note: Discounting/valuation impairments associated with substance use; findings mixed, consistent with original 'mixed' caveat.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Delay->Substance Abuse + p.6 'robust links to the HiTOP Disinhibited Externalizing spectrum and its Substance Abuse...subfactor...greater delay discounting with impulsivity...substance use'
verification_note: Systematic review: delay discounting robustly associated with substance use; greater discounting = impaired valuation (-1).

source: Fig2 blue arrow Delay->Antisocial Behavior + p.6 '...and Antisocial Behavior subfactors, respectively supported by associations of greater delay discounting with...antisocial behavior'
verification_note: Greater delay discounting/impulsive choice associated with externalizing-antisocial behavior; same -1 direction.

source: Fig2 red arrow Effort->Substance Abuse + p.6 'link with HiTOP Substance Abuse, as indicated by associations of addiction with heightened self-reported drive and rash effort-based decision making'
verification_note: No independent meta-analysis located linking heightened effort/drive specifically to substance abuse in the +1 direction.

source: Fig2 blue arrow Effort->Distress + p.6 'link to the HiTOP Distress subfactor via deficient willingness to exert effort and decreased drive...with depressive symptoms, particularly anhedonia'
verification_note: Effort-based decision deficits strongly tied to amotivation/anhedonia (effort discounting r=-.44); supports reduced-effort (-1) with distress/anhedonia.

source: p.6 'additional link...with HiTOP Thought Disorder via associations of negative symptoms of schizophrenia with impaired effort-based decision making' (Fig2 shows blue arrows to Distress/Substance Abuse; Thought Disorder via text)
verification_note: Meta-analysis: effort-discounting tasks most strongly related to schizophrenia negative symptoms (r=-.44); matches impaired-effort (-1).

source: Fig3 blue arrow Attention->Disinhibited Externalizing + p.6-7 'robust association between markers of RDoC Attention and the HiTOP Disinhibited Externalizing spectrum...TPN-DMN imbalance...inattentive, hyperactive-impulsive symptoms'
verification_note: Reduced ERN/attentional-control markers tied to externalizing (ADHD/impulsivity); supports impaired-attention (-1) with disinhibition.

source: Fig3 blue arrow Attention->Substance Abuse + p.7 'link to the Substance Abuse subfactor...attentional impairments and substance use'
verification_note: Attentional/impulse-control impairments associated with substance use; consistent with -1 direction.

source: Fig3 blue arrow Attention->Thought Disorder + p.7 'robust association...between RDoC Attention and the HiTOP Thought Disorder...attentional dysfunction...with symptoms of schizophrenia and positive schizotypy'
verification_note: First-episode schizophrenia shows broad neurocognitive deficits including attention; matches impaired-attention (-1) in Thought Disorder.

source: p.7 'RDoC Attention may also be linked with HiTOP Detachment, based on associations of poor performance in sustained attention tasks with negative schizotypy'
verification_note: No independent meta-analysis located specifically on sustained attention with negative schizotypy/Detachment.

source: p.7 'emerging association...with the HiTOP Distress subfactor...increased reaction time variability, distractibility with depression and suicidality'
verification_note: Attention/executive deficits associated with depression and suicidality transdiagnostically; consistent with -1, though mixed across domains.

source: Fig3 blue arrow Visual Perception->Thought Disorder + p.7 'RDoC Perception shows a clear association with HiTOP Thought Disorder...attenuated visual ERPs...with symptoms of schizophrenia, positive schizotypy'
verification_note: Early sensory/perceptual processing deficits are well established in schizophrenia; consistent with attenuated visual perception (-1).

source: Fig3 blue arrow Auditory Perception->Thought Disorder + p.7 'attenuated auditory...ERPs (e.g., reduced mismatch negativity, P50, N1, P2, P3) with symptoms of schizophrenia'
verification_note: Meta-analysis: reduced mismatch negativity in schizophrenia (large in chronic, duration-MMN d=0.47 first-episode); matches attenuated auditory perception (-1).

source: p.7 'limited evidence...link RDoC Olfactory/Somatosensory/Multimodal Perception...emerging link with HiTOP Thought Disorder...olfactory perceptual deficits and psychotic symptoms' (Fig3 shows no arrow from Olfactory)

source: Fig3 blue arrow Declarative Memory->Thought Disorder + p.7 'robust associations with HiTOP Thought Disorder...behavioral deficits and atypical hippocampal function...with psychotic disorders'

source: p.7 'potential link with the HiTOP Distress subfactor...behavioral markers of episodic memory and depressive symptoms'

source: Fig3 blue arrow Language->Thought Disorder + p.7 'robust link with HiTOP Thought Disorder...alterations in brain circuits underlying verbal fluency, language...with psychotic symptoms'

rdoc_subconstruct: Goal Selection; Updating, Representation, and Maintenance

source: Fig3 red arrow Performance Monitoring<->Fear + p.7 'robust link to HiTOP Fear, based on...higher error-related negativity (ERN)...with OCD traits...and social anxiety/shyness'

source: Fig3 blue arrow Performance Monitoring->Disinhibited Externalizing + p.7 'well-supported link with the HiTOP Disinhibited Externalizing spectrum, via associations of reduced post-error slowing, ERN amplitude...with measures of disinhibition, impulsivity, inattention, hyperactivity'

source: Fig5 blue arrow Arousal->Disinhibited Externalizing + p.9 'robust link to the HiTOP Disinhibited Externalizing spectrum...low skin conductance during a low-demand attentional task...with inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity...hypo-arousal as a core underpinning'

source: Fig5 blue arrow Arousal->Antisocial Behavior + p.9 'Antisocial Behavior subfactor at the intersection of the HiTOP Disinhibited and Antagonistic spectra also shows robust associations...low skin conductance...low pupil dilation...with antisocial behavior and aggression'

source: Fig5 blue arrow Sleep-Wakefulness<->Distress + p.9 'robust link with the HiTOP Distress subfactor, based on associations of insomnia and low sleep quality with depression and anxiety'

source: p.9 '...HiTOP Disinhibited Externalizing...Antisocial Behavior subfactors, as lower sleep quality...with externalizing problems'

source: LeMon JV et al., 2019, Physiology & Behavior; PMID 30858100; DOI 10.1016/j.physbeh.2019.03.005; de Jong JW et al., 2013, PLoS ONE; PMID 24058616; DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0074645
evidence_note: Binge-eating-prone rats fail to suppress responding for a reinforcer devalued by sensory-specific satiety (insensitivity to reward devaluation/satiation), i.e. impaired satiation drives addiction-like overconsumption. Deficit pattern: lower satiation function maps to more disinhibited/addiction-like behavior, hence -1. Closest in-scope column is Disinhibited Externalizing/Substance Abuse via the food-addiction framing; HiTOP Eating Pathology (the most direct target) is NOT an in-scope column here. | Rats with low control over palatable food intake (addiction-like: high motivation, persistence despite aversive consequences) were less sensitive to reward devaluation and more prone to relapse/reinstatement; reduced satiation-driven devaluation co-occurs with habitual, addiction-like consumption. Impaired satiation maps to more substance/addiction-like externalizing pathology, hence -1.
verification_citation: LeMon JV et al., 2019, Physiology & Behavior; PMID 30858100; DOI 10.1016/j.physbeh.2019.03.005; de Jong JW et al., 2013, PLoS ONE; PMID 24058616; DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0074645

source: Serretti A, 2023, Clinical Psychopharmacology and Neuroscience; PMID 37424409; DOI 10.9758/cpn.23.1086; Borsini A et al., 2020, Cognitive Affective & Behavioral Neuroscience; PMID 32472419; DOI 10.3758/s13415-020-00804-6
evidence_note: Anhedonia (a spectrum of reward-processing deficits including blunted reward liking/consummatory pleasure, i.e. diminished hedonic responsiveness) is a core symptom of depressive disorders and a transdiagnostic internalizing feature. Blunted hedonic/satiation-related reward function maps to more depression/distress, hence deficit-pattern -1. | Systematic review of 59 fMRI studies: MDD shows striatal hypoactivation in reward liking/wanting and blunted frontostriatal sensitivity, characterizing anhedonia as blunted hedonic/reward responsiveness. Impaired hedonic reward function maps to more depressive (Internalizing/Distress) pathology, hence -1.
verification_citation: Serretti A, 2023, Clinical Psychopharmacology and Neuroscience; PMID 37424409; DOI 10.9758/cpn.23.1086; Borsini A et al., 2020, Cognitive Affective & Behavioral Neuroscience; PMID 32472419; DOI 10.3758/s13415-020-00804-6

source: Dollish HK, Tsyglakova M, McClung CA, 2023, Neuron; PMID 37858331; DOI 10.1016/j.neuron.2023.09.023; Kivela L, Papadopoulos MR, Antypa N, 2018, Current Sleep Medicine Reports; PMID 29888167; DOI 10.1007/s40675-018-0113-8
evidence_note: Review: all mood disorders (SAD, MDD, bipolar) are strongly associated with abnormal/disrupted circadian rhythms; circadian and light/dark disruptions precipitate depressive episodes and circadian-targeting therapies are antidepressant. Weaker/disrupted circadian regulation maps to more depression/distress, hence -1. Bipolar/mania has strong circadian ties but is not a clean in-scope HiTOP column. | Review: eveningness/circadian misalignment is well established cross-sectionally with depression including seasonal affective disorder, with longitudinal support; support for anxiety disorders and psychosis was weaker. In bipolar disorder chronotype is linked to depression but not mania (noted: mania not an in-scope column). Disrupted circadian regulation maps to more depressive/internalizing pathology, hence -1.
verification_citation: Dollish HK, Tsyglakova M, McClung CA, 2023, Neuron; PMID 37858331; DOI 10.1016/j.neuron.2023.09.023; Kivela L, Papadopoulos MR, Antypa N, 2018, Current Sleep Medicine Reports; PMID 29888167; DOI 10.1007/s40675-018-0113-8

source: Meyer N et al., 2024, PNAS; PMID 38394243; DOI 10.1073/pnas.2214756121; Moon E, Lavin P, Storch KF, Linnaranta O, 2020, Progress in Neuro-Psychopharmacology & Biological Psychiatry; PMID 33152385; DOI 10.1016/j.pnpbp.2020.110162
evidence_note: Review framing sleep-circadian dysfunction as a transdiagnostic contributor across mood, anxiety, and psychosis-spectrum disorders, integrating circadian disruption into psychosis pathophysiology. Disrupted circadian regulation maps to more thought-disorder/psychosis pathology, hence -1. | Systematic review/meta-analysis: schizophrenia patients show transdiagnostic circadian abnormalities (decreased amplitude of cortisol, melatonin, body-temperature rhythms); high prevalence and unfavorable impact of disrupted circadian rhythms in psychotic illness. Weaker/disrupted circadian rhythmicity maps to more thought-disorder pathology, hence -1.
verification_citation: Meyer N et al., 2024, PNAS; PMID 38394243; DOI 10.1073/pnas.2214756121; Moon E, Lavin P, Storch KF, Linnaranta O, 2020, Progress in Neuro-Psychopharmacology & Biological Psychiatry; PMID 33152385; DOI 10.1016/j.pnpbp.2020.110162

source: Steardo L Jr et al., 2025, Sleep Medicine Reviews; PMID 40570562; DOI 10.1016/j.smrv.2025.102116; Meneo D et al., 2023, Sleep Medicine Reviews; PMID 37269785; DOI 10.1016/j.smrv.2023.101792
evidence_note: Systematic review with meta-analysis (5 studies): evening preference confers significantly higher odds of substance use disorder vs morning preference (pooled OR=1.55, 95% CI 1.20-1.95), plus earlier onset, greater severity, poorer outcomes. Eveningness reflects circadian misalignment/weaker regulation; disrupted circadian function maps to more substance-abuse pathology, hence -1. | Systematic review and meta-analysis (46 studies) of young adults: evening chronotype was associated with alcohol, caffeine, and nicotine use. Eveningness/circadian misalignment maps to more substance use, hence deficit-pattern -1.
verification_citation: Steardo L Jr et al., 2025, Sleep Medicine Reviews; PMID 40570562; DOI 10.1016/j.smrv.2025.102116; Meneo D et al., 2023, Sleep Medicine Reviews; PMID 37269785; DOI 10.1016/j.smrv.2023.101792

source: Bora et al. 2018, Eur Neuropsychopharmacol; PMID 30170709; DOI 10.1016/j.euroneuro.2018.08.006
evidence_note: Neurological soft signs (subtle deficits in motor coordination, sensory integration and sequencing of complex motor acts) are robustly elevated in schizophrenia (d up to ~1.5) and bipolar disorder. Deficit pattern: impaired/abnormal motor function tracks more psychosis-spectrum pathology, hence -1.
verification_citation: Bora et al. 2018, Eur Neuropsychopharmacol; PMID 30170709; DOI 10.1016/j.euroneuro.2018.08.006

source: Wuthrich et al. 2022, Psychol Med; PMID 35550677; DOI 10.1017/S0033291722000903
evidence_note: Actigraphy meta-analysis (34 studies, n=1804): depressed/bipolar patients show reduced motor activity vs controls (psychomotor slowing/retardation), even in remission. Reduced motor output (lower function) tracks more Distress, hence -1.
verification_citation: Wuthrich et al. 2022, Psychol Med; PMID 35550677; DOI 10.1017/S0033291722000903

source: Hirjak et al. (NSS literature) / Bora et al. 2018, Eur Neuropsychopharmacol; PMID 30170709; DOI 10.1016/j.euroneuro.2018.08.006
evidence_note: Sequencing of complex motor acts (fist-edge-palm, fist-ring, Ozeretski tasks) is a core NSS domain impaired in schizophrenia; reflects deficient motor planning/selection. Impairment tracks more Thought Disorder, hence -1.
verification_citation: Hirjak et al. (NSS literature) / Bora et al. 2018, Eur Neuropsychopharmacol; PMID 30170709; DOI 10.1016/j.euroneuro.2018.08.006

source: Bora et al. 2018, Eur Neuropsychopharmacol; PMID 30170709; DOI 10.1016/j.euroneuro.2018.08.006
evidence_note: Sensory integration / sensorimotor coordination deficits form a robust NSS domain in schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, present in neuroleptic-naive first-episode patients. Deficit pattern -> -1 with Thought Disorder.
verification_citation: Bora et al. 2018, Eur Neuropsychopharmacol; PMID 30170709; DOI 10.1016/j.euroneuro.2018.08.006

source: Caroff et al. 2022, Schizophr Res; PMID 36404216; DOI 10.1016/j.schres.2022.11.015
evidence_note: Catatonia (akinesia, mutism, impaired motor initiation/abulia) is a psychomotor syndrome occurring in schizophrenia; impaired initiation of action is a marker of psychosis-spectrum severity, hence -1 with Thought Disorder.
verification_citation: Caroff et al. 2022, Schizophr Res; PMID 36404216; DOI 10.1016/j.schres.2022.11.015

source: Wuthrich et al. 2022, Psychol Med; PMID 35550677; DOI 10.1017/S0033291722000903
evidence_note: Psychomotor retardation in depression includes reduced initiation/latency of movement (lower activity on actigraphy). Lower initiation tracks more Distress, hence -1.
verification_citation: Wuthrich et al. 2022, Psychol Med; PMID 35550677; DOI 10.1017/S0033291722000903

source: Bora et al. 2018, Eur Neuropsychopharmacol; PMID 30170709; DOI 10.1016/j.euroneuro.2018.08.006
evidence_note: Abnormal motor execution / dyskinetic and coordination NSS are well-established markers of schizophrenia (prevalence >50% vs ~5% in controls) and correlate with psychosis severity. Impaired execution -> -1 with Thought Disorder.
verification_citation: Bora et al. 2018, Eur Neuropsychopharmacol; PMID 30170709; DOI 10.1016/j.euroneuro.2018.08.006

source: Lipszyc & Schachar 2010, J Int Neuropsychol Soc (stop-signal meta-analysis); PMID 20719043; DOI 10.1017/S1355617710000895
evidence_note: Stop-signal task meta-analysis: medium SSRT deficits in ADHD (g=0.62), reflecting impaired motor response inhibition central to disinhibitory externalizing psychopathology. Impaired inhibition -> -1.
verification_citation: Lipszyc & Schachar 2010, J Int Neuropsychol Soc (stop-signal meta-analysis); PMID 20719043; DOI 10.1017/S1355617710000895

source: Pasion et al. 2019, Rev Neurosci (N2/antisocial meta-analysis); PMID 30954973; DOI 10.1515/revneuro-2018-0116
evidence_note: Reduced N2 (motor-inhibition ERP) across the antisocial spectrum; inhibition was the core deficit explaining blunted N2 amplitudes in antisocial/impulsive behavior. Impaired motor inhibition -> -1 with Antisocial Behavior.
verification_citation: Pasion et al. 2019, Rev Neurosci (N2/antisocial meta-analysis); PMID 30954973; DOI 10.1515/revneuro-2018-0116

source: Lipszyc & Schachar 2010, J Int Neuropsychol Soc; PMID 20719043; DOI 10.1017/S1355617710000895
evidence_note: Same stop-signal meta-analysis found medium SSRT deficits in schizophrenia (g=0.69), indicating impaired motor inhibition in psychosis. Deficit pattern -> -1 with Thought Disorder.
verification_citation: Lipszyc & Schachar 2010, J Int Neuropsychol Soc; PMID 20719043; DOI 10.1017/S1355617710000895

source: Malik et al. 2022, Neurosci Biobehav Rev; PMID 35780975; DOI 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2022.104759
evidence_note: Comprehensive review: aberrant sense of agency (passivity phenomena, delusions of control) is characteristic of schizophrenia; disrupted agency for one's own actions maps to the Thought Disorder spectrum. Impaired/anomalous agency -> -1.
verification_citation: Malik et al. 2022, Neurosci Biobehav Rev; PMID 35780975; DOI 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2022.104759

source: Malik et al. 2022, Neurosci Biobehav Rev; PMID 35780975; DOI 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2022.104759
evidence_note: Anomalous agency and ownership (depersonalization-like disturbances of self-experience) relate to detachment/dissociative self-disturbance; diminished sense of agency tracks more Detachment, hence -1.
verification_citation: Malik et al. 2022, Neurosci Biobehav Rev; PMID 35780975; DOI 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2022.104759

source: Maurer et al. 2016, NeuroImage Clin / Nahab et al. 2017 (impaired agency in functional movement disorders); PMC5407819
evidence_note: fMRI evidence of impaired sense of agency in functional movement disorders (DSM-5 functional neurological/conversion disorder, a somatoform-spectrum condition): movements feel involuntary due to prediction-outcome mismatch and reduced sensory attenuation. Loss of agency -> -1 with Somatoform.
verification_citation: Maurer et al. 2016, NeuroImage Clin / Nahab et al. 2017 (impaired agency in functional movement disorders); PMC5407819

source: Everitt & Robbins / Lüscher et al. addiction habit-compulsivity reviews (substance use disorder habit learning)
evidence_note: Addiction models posit a shift from goal-directed control to stimulus-response (sensorimotor) habit learning that drives compulsive drug seeking. Stronger/overactive habit system tracks more substance abuse, hence +1.
verification_citation: Everitt & Robbins / Lüscher et al. addiction habit-compulsivity reviews (substance use disorder habit learning)

source: Gillan & Robbins compulsivity/habit reviews (habit learning in compulsive disorders)
evidence_note: Excessive reliance on the sensorimotor habit system over goal-directed control is implicated in compulsive/disinhibited behavior more broadly. Overactive habit learning -> +1 with Disinhibited Externalizing.
verification_citation: Gillan & Robbins compulsivity/habit reviews (habit learning in compulsive disorders)

source: Caroff et al. 2022, Schizophr Res; PMID 36404216; DOI 10.1016/j.schres.2022.11.015
evidence_note: Catatonia involves disturbance of innate/automatic motor patterns (posturing, stereotypy, mannerisms, waxy flexibility, primitive reflexes/grasping) in schizophrenia; disrupted innate motor patterning marks psychosis-spectrum pathology, hence -1 with Thought Disorder.
verification_citation: Caroff et al. 2022, Schizophr Res; PMID 36404216; DOI 10.1016/j.schres.2022.11.015
